problem_description: A citizen disputes the boundary of their agricultural or residential land with a neighbouring landowner. The neighbour may have encroached on the land, moved boundary markers, or built a structure on disputed land.
workflow_steps: Step 1 – Collect all original land documents including the 7/12 extract (Satbara), property card, and old survey maps from the Talathi office.
Step 2 – Take dated photographs of the disputed boundary, encroachment, or construction.
Step 3 – Speak to local Panch members or village elders to gather witness support.
Step 4 – Visit the Talathi office and request an official land record inspection and boundary demarcation.
Step 5 – File a written complaint with the Tehsildar if the Talathi does not resolve the matter within 15 days.
Step 6 – The Tehsildar will conduct a revenue inquiry and may appoint a surveyor for boundary re-demarcation.
Step 7 – If still unresolved, file a civil suit in the Civil Court with the help of a lawyer.
Step 8 – Approach the District Legal Services Authority (DLSA) for free legal aid if you cannot afford a lawyer.
required_documents: Original sale deed or gift deed
Old survey map or cadastral map
Witness statements from neighbours or Panch members
escalation_path: Talathi Office → Tehsildar Office → Sub-Divisional Magistrate → District Collector → Civil Court
complaint_template:
To,
The Tehsildar,
[Taluka Name], [District Name]

Subject: Complaint regarding land boundary dispute

Respected Sir/Madam,

I, [Your Name], residing at [Your Address], am writing to file a complaint regarding a boundary dispute with my neighbour [Neighbour's Name] concerning land bearing Survey Number [Survey No.] in village [Village Name].

The neighbouring party has encroached upon my land and shifted the boundary markers without legal authority. Despite repeated requests for resolution, the matter remains unresolved.

I request your kind intervention to conduct a revenue inquiry and official boundary demarcation at the earliest.

Enclosures: 7/12 extract, property card, photographs

Yours faithfully,
[Name]
[Address]
[Date]
[Mobile Number]
online_portals: State Revenue Department portal (varies by state)
helplines: District Collector Office: Contact local office

problem_description: A citizen's land has been illegally occupied by another person, builder, or party. The occupant may have erected structures, fencing, or is using the land without legal ownership.
workflow_steps: Step 1 – Gather all ownership documents including sale deed, 7/12 extract, and property card.
Step 2 – Photograph the encroachment and note the date it began.
Step 3 – Send a written legal notice to the encroaching party demanding vacating of land.
Step 4 – File a complaint with the local police station if the encroachment involves trespass or threat.
Step 5 – File a complaint with the Tehsildar office requesting removal of encroachment from government or private land.
Step 6 – Approach the Sub-Divisional Magistrate if the Tehsildar does not act within 30 days.
Step 7 – File a civil suit for recovery of possession in Civil Court.
Step 8 – Contact the District Legal Services Authority for free legal assistance.
required_documents: Written legal notice sent to encroacher
escalation_path: Local Police Station → Tehsildar → Sub-Divisional Magistrate → District Collector → Civil Court
complaint_template:
To,
The Tehsildar,
[Taluka Name], [District Name]

Subject: Complaint regarding illegal occupation of my land

Respected Sir/Madam,

I, [Your Name], residing at [Your Address], own land bearing Survey Number [Survey No.] in village [Village Name] as per registered sale deed dated [Date]. My land has been illegally occupied by [Encroacher's Name] since [Date], who has constructed/fenced/used the land without my consent or legal right.

I have already issued a written notice to the encroacher which was not complied with.

I request your authority to take immediate action to remove the illegal encroachment and restore possession of my land.

Enclosures: Sale deed, 7/12 extract, photographs, copy of legal notice

Yours faithfully,
[Name]
[Address]
[Date]
[Mobile Number]

subcategory: Land Record Error / Wrong Entry in Land Records
problem_description: A citizen's name is incorrectly recorded in land records (7/12, property card), or there is a wrong entry for land area, type, or ownership due to clerical error or fraud.
workflow_steps: Step 1 – Obtain the current 7/12 extract and property card from the Talathi office or online land record portal.
Step 2 – Identify the specific error (name, survey number, area, ownership).
Step 3 – Collect supporting documents that prove the correct information (sale deed, old records, tax receipts).
Step 4 – Submit an application to the Talathi requesting correction of the land record with supporting documents.
Step 5 – If the Talathi does not correct the record within 30 days, escalate to the Tehsildar.
Step 6 – File a formal grievance with the District Collector if Tehsildar fails to act.
Step 7 – Approach the Revenue Tribunal or Civil Court for judicial correction of records if the administrative route fails.
required_documents: Original sale deed or title document
Old land records showing correct information
escalation_path: Talathi → Tehsildar → District Collector → Revenue Tribunal → Civil Court
complaint_template:
To,
The Talathi,
[Village Name], [Taluka], [District]

Subject: Application for correction of error in land records

Respected Sir/Madam,

I, [Your Name], am the owner of land bearing Survey Number [Survey No.] in village [Village Name]. I have noticed that the land records (7/12 extract) contain an error wherein [describe error, e.g., my name is incorrectly spelled as / land area is wrongly recorded as / ownership is wrongly shown as].

The correct information as per my registered sale deed dated [Date] is [correct information].

I request you to kindly correct this error in the land records at the earliest.

Enclosures: Copy of current 7/12 extract, copy of sale deed, identity proof

Yours faithfully,
[Name]
[Address]
[Date]
[Mobile Number]

subcategory: Fraudulent Sale or Transfer of Property
problem_description: A citizen's property has been fraudulently sold, transferred, or mortgaged without their knowledge or consent, often using forged documents.
workflow_steps: Step 1 – Obtain a certified copy of the allegedly fraudulent sale deed from the Sub-Registrar office.
Step 2 – Check the land records to see if ownership has been transferred without your knowledge.
Step 3 – File a First Information Report (FIR) at the local police station for fraud and forgery.
Step 4 – Submit a complaint to the Sub-Registrar reporting the fraudulent registration.
Step 5 – File a complaint with the Tehsildar to freeze any further transfer of the property.
Step 6 – Engage a lawyer to file a civil suit for cancellation of fraudulent sale deed.
Step 7 – Report to the District Collector for an inquiry into the revenue records fraud.
Step 8 – Approach the High Court with a writ petition if the civil court process is delayed and urgency exists.
required_documents: Original title documents (sale deed, gift deed)
Certified copy of fraudulent sale deed
escalation_path: Police Station → Sub-Registrar → Tehsildar → District Collector → Civil Court → High Court
complaint_template:
To,
The Sub-Inspector / Station House Officer,
[Police Station Name]

Subject: FIR complaint regarding fraudulent sale of my property

Respected Sir/Madam,

I, [Your Name], am the legal owner of property at [Property Address] bearing Survey No. [Survey No.]. I have recently discovered that my property has been fraudulently sold/transferred to [Unknown Person / Name] using forged documents without my knowledge or consent.

This amounts to criminal fraud and forgery under Sections 420 and 468 of the Indian Penal Code.

I request you to kindly register an FIR and investigate this matter immediately.

Enclosures: Original title documents, copy of fraudulent deed, 7/12 extract

Yours faithfully,
[Name]
[Address]
[Date]
[Mobile Number]

problem_description: After the death of a family member, legal heirs dispute ownership of the inherited property, or one heir illegally takes possession excluding other legal heirs.
workflow_steps: Step 1 – Obtain the death certificate of the deceased family member.
Step 2 – Collect all property documents in the deceased's name.
Step 3 – Identify all legal heirs as per Hindu Succession Act or applicable personal law.
Step 4 – Apply for mutation of land records in the Talathi office in the names of all legal heirs.
Step 5 – If an heir is excluded or refusing, approach the Tehsildar for a succession inquiry.
Step 6 – Obtain a legal heirship certificate from the Tahsildar or court.
Step 7 – File a civil suit for partition of property if heirs cannot agree.
Step 8 – Approach DLSA for free legal aid if you cannot afford a lawyer.
escalation_path: Talathi → Tehsildar → Civil Court → High Court
complaint_template:
To,
The Tehsildar,
[Taluka], [District]

Subject: Application for mutation of land records following death of property owner

Respected Sir/Madam,

I, [Your Name], am the legal heir of late [Deceased Name] who passed away on [Date]. The deceased owned land bearing Survey No. [Survey No.] in village [Village Name].

I request you to kindly initiate the mutation process and update the land records in the names of all legal heirs as per succession laws.

Enclosures: Death certificate, property documents, identity proof, relationship documents

Yours faithfully,
[Name]
[Address]
[Date]
[Mobile Number]

problem_description: A person's house or structure has been illegally demolished by a builder, landlord, government body, or private party without due legal process or notice.
workflow_steps: Step 1 – Document the demolition with photographs and video evidence immediately.
Step 2 – Collect all documents proving ownership or tenancy of the demolished structure.
Step 3 – File a police complaint at the local police station for destruction of property.
Step 4 – File a complaint with the Municipal Corporation or Gram Panchayat if the demolition was done by a local body without proper notice.
Step 5 – Approach the Sub-Divisional Magistrate for urgent relief and stay orders.
Step 6 – File a writ petition in the High Court if rights are violated without due process.
Step 7 – Seek compensation through Civil Court for damages caused.
escalation_path: Police Station → Municipal Corporation → SDM → District Collector → High Court
complaint_template:
To,
The Sub-Divisional Magistrate,
[Sub-Division Name], [District]

Subject: Complaint regarding illegal demolition of my property

Respected Sir/Madam,

I, [Your Name], residing at [Address], wish to report that my property at [Location] was illegally demolished on [Date] by [Party Name / Authority] without any prior legal notice or court order.

This demolition has caused me severe hardship and financial loss. I request your urgent intervention to investigate this matter and provide appropriate relief and compensation.

Enclosures: Ownership documents, photographs, identity proof

Yours faithfully,
[Name]
[Address]
[Date]
[Mobile Number]

problem_description: Agricultural land is being converted to non-agricultural use without proper permission, causing disputes between landowners, farmers, and authorities.
workflow_steps: Step 1 – Verify current land classification in the 7/12 extract.
Step 2 – Collect evidence of unauthorized conversion (construction, change of use).
Step 3 – File a complaint with the Tehsildar about unauthorized land use change.
Step 4 – Report to the District Collector if conversion is being done illegally.
Step 5 – Approach the Agriculture Department if agricultural land is being misused.
Step 6 – File a complaint with the state Town Planning Authority if zoning laws are violated.
Step 7 – Seek legal remedy through Civil Court or Revenue Tribunal if administrative remedy fails.
required_documents: Photographs of unauthorized construction or conversion
escalation_path: Talathi → Tehsildar → District Collector → State Agriculture Department → Revenue Tribunal
complaint_template:
To,
The Tehsildar,
[Taluka], [District]

Subject: Complaint regarding unauthorized conversion of agricultural land

Respected Sir/Madam,

I, [Your Name], wish to report that agricultural land bearing Survey No. [Survey No.] in village [Village Name] is being illegally converted to non-agricultural use by [Party Name] without obtaining required permissions under the Land Revenue Code.

I request you to take immediate action to stop this unauthorized conversion and restore the land to its original agricultural use.

Enclosures: 7/12 extract, photographs, identity proof

Yours faithfully,
[Name]
[Address]
[Date]
[Mobile Number]

problem_description: After divorce, spouses dispute ownership of shared or matrimonial property including house, land, or other immovable assets.
workflow_steps: Step 1 – Collect all property documents, purchase receipts, and financial contribution records.
Step 2 – Identify property acquired before and during marriage.
Step 3 – Approach mediation through Family Court or DLSA for an amicable settlement.
Step 4 – File a petition in Family Court for division of matrimonial property if mediation fails.
Step 5 – Provide evidence of contribution to the property (joint loan documents, bank statements).
Step 6 – The Family Court will decide division based on applicable personal or general law.
Step 7 – Approach the High Court in appeal if dissatisfied with the Family Court order.
escalation_path: DLSA Mediation → Family Court → High Court
complaint_template:
To,
The Principal Judge,
Family Court,
[City / District]

Subject: Petition for division of matrimonial property

Respected Sir/Madam,

I, [Your Name], am the petitioner in the divorce matter against [Spouse Name]. I wish to submit that the matrimonial property at [Address] was jointly acquired during our marriage and I have equal financial contribution towards its purchase.

I request the court to pass appropriate orders for fair division of the said property.

Yours faithfully,
[Name]
[Address]
[Date]

problem_description: The government acquires a citizen's land for public projects without proper compensation, notification, or legal process under the Right to Fair Compensation Act.
workflow_steps: Step 1 – Receive and carefully read the official acquisition notice from the government.
Step 2 – Collect all land ownership documents and market value assessment records.
Step 3 – Attend the public hearing called by the Land Acquisition Officer.
Step 4 – Submit a written objection to the Land Acquisition Officer if compensation is inadequate.
Step 5 – Apply to the Land Acquisition Compensation Tribunal for enhanced compensation.
Step 6 – File a writ petition in High Court if procedural violations occurred during acquisition.
Step 7 – Seek assistance from DLSA for free legal representation.
required_documents: Objection letter filed with Land Acquisition Officer
escalation_path: Land Acquisition Officer → District Collector → Compensation Tribunal → High Court
complaint_template:
To,
The Land Acquisition Officer,
[District]

Subject: Objection to land acquisition and demand for fair compensation

Respected Sir/Madam,

I, [Your Name], am the owner of land bearing Survey No. [Survey No.] in village [Village Name]. I have received an acquisition notice dated [Date] for the [Project Name].

I wish to object that the proposed compensation of Rs. [Amount] is far below the market value of my land which is approximately Rs. [Market Value]. I request a review of the compensation amount in accordance with the Right to Fair Compensation and Transparency in Land Acquisition Act, 2013.

Enclosures: Land documents, market value records, identity proof

Yours faithfully,
[Name]
[Address]
[Date]
[Mobile Number]

subcategory: False Mutation or Unauthorized Change in Land Ownership Record
problem_description: A citizen discovers that land records have been fraudulently mutated in someone else's name without their knowledge, often with the connivance of revenue officials.
workflow_steps: Step 1 – Obtain latest 7/12 extract and property card to verify the unauthorized mutation.
Step 2 – Collect all original ownership documents disproving the mutation.
Step 3 – File a complaint with the Tehsildar requesting cancellation of fraudulent mutation.
Step 4 – File a police complaint for fraud and forgery at the local police station.
Step 5 – Report to the District Collector with evidence of the fraudulent mutation.
Step 6 – File a Revenue Tribunal case for cancellation of the mutated entry.
Step 7 – If revenue officials are involved, file complaint with the Anti-Corruption Bureau.
Step 8 – Approach Civil Court for final adjudication of title.
required_documents: Original sale deed and title documents
Previous land records showing correct ownership
escalation_path: Tehsildar → District Collector → Revenue Tribunal → Anti-Corruption Bureau → Civil Court
complaint_template:
To,
The District Collector,
[District]

Subject: Complaint regarding fraudulent mutation of land records

Respected Sir/Madam,

I, [Your Name], am the legal owner of land bearing Survey No. [Survey No.] in village [Village Name]. I have discovered that the land records have been fraudulently mutated in the name of [Fraudster Name] without my knowledge or consent.

I have all original ownership documents and this mutation is clearly fraudulent. I request you to immediately cancel the fraudulent mutation and restore the correct records in my name.

Enclosures: Original title deed, current 7/12 extract, identity proof

Yours faithfully,
[Name]
[Address]
[Date]
[Mobile Number]

problem_description: An employee has not received their salary for one or more months despite working. The employer is withholding wages without valid reason.
workflow_steps: Step 1 – Collect all evidence of employment: appointment letter, salary slips, attendance records, bank statements.
Step 2 – Send a written reminder or demand letter to the employer requesting payment of due salary.
Step 3 – If no response within 15 days, file a complaint with the Labour Commissioner office in your district.
Step 4 – The Labour Commissioner will call both parties for a conciliation hearing.
Step 5 – If conciliation fails, the Labour Commissioner will refer the matter to the Labour Court.
Step 6 – File an application in the Labour Court under the Payment of Wages Act for recovery of wages.
Step 7 – The court will direct the employer to pay the wages plus compensation.
Step 8 – Approach DLSA for free legal aid if needed.
required_documents: Bank account statements showing last salary received
escalation_path: Labour Commissioner → Conciliation → Labour Court → High Court
complaint_template:
To,
The Labour Commissioner,
[District]

Subject: Complaint regarding non-payment of salary

Respected Sir/Madam,

I, [Your Name], was employed as [Designation] at [Company Name], [Address] from [Joining Date]. I have not received my salary for the months of [Months] totalling Rs. [Amount] despite repeated verbal and written requests to my employer.

I request your office to intervene and direct my employer to release the pending salary at the earliest.

Enclosures: Appointment letter, salary slips, bank statements, demand letter

Yours faithfully,
[Name]
[Address]
[Date]
[Mobile Number]

problem_description: An employee has been illegally terminated without following due process, without payment of dues, or without valid legal reasons as required under labour laws.
workflow_steps: Step 1 – Collect the termination letter and all employment-related documents.
Step 2 – Note whether notice period and severance pay were provided as legally required.
Step 3 – Send a legal notice to the employer demanding reinstatement or proper settlement.
Step 4 – File a complaint with the Labour Commissioner for conciliation.
Step 5 – Raise an industrial dispute under the Industrial Disputes Act at the Labour Commissioner office.
Step 6 – If conciliation fails, approach the Labour Court or Industrial Tribunal.
Step 7 – The court may order reinstatement with back wages or compensation.
Step 8 – Contact DLSA for free legal representation.
required_documents: Any written warnings or show-cause notices received
escalation_path: Labour Commissioner (Conciliation) → Labour Court → Industrial Tribunal → High Court
complaint_template:
To,
The Labour Commissioner,
[District]

Subject: Complaint regarding wrongful termination of employment

Respected Sir/Madam,

I, [Your Name], was employed as [Designation] at [Company Name] from [Date] to [Date of Termination]. I was terminated without proper notice, without payment of dues, and without valid legal reason. My termination was not in accordance with the Industrial Disputes Act, 1947.

I request your office to mediate this dispute and direct my employer to either reinstate me or provide full legal dues and compensation.

Enclosures: Appointment letter, termination letter, salary slips, identity proof

Yours faithfully,
[Name]
[Address]
[Date]
[Mobile Number]

problem_description: A beneficiary who worked under the Mahatma Gandhi National Rural Employment Guarantee Act (MNREGA) has not received their wages within the legally mandated 15 days.
workflow_steps: Step 1 – Obtain your MNREGA Job Card and verify the work done and wages due.
Step 2 – Check if your Aadhaar and bank account are linked to the MNREGA portal.
Step 3 – File a complaint with the Gram Panchayat secretary requesting immediate wage payment.
Step 4 – If no payment within 7 days, file a complaint with the Block Development Officer (BDO).
Step 5 – The BDO will investigate and may direct the Gram Panchayat to release wages.
Step 6 – If the BDO is non-responsive, escalate to the District Programme Coordinator (DPC), who is usually the District Collector.
Step 7 – File a complaint on the MNREGA Grievance Portal online.
Step 8 – Approach the State Employment Guarantee Council if district-level resolution fails.
required_documents: Muster rolls showing attendance and work done
escalation_path: Gram Panchayat → Block Development Officer → District Programme Coordinator → State Employment Guarantee Council → Ministry of Rural Development
complaint_template:
To,
The Block Development Officer,
[Block Name], [District]

Subject: Complaint regarding delayed MNREGA wage payment

Respected Sir/Madam,

I, [Your Name], am a Job Card holder under MNREGA (Job Card No. [Job Card No.]). I worked from [Date] to [Date] at [Work Site Name] in village [Village Name] but have not received my wages of Rs. [Amount] even after [Number] days, which violates the 15-day payment norm.

I request you to investigate and ensure my wages are paid immediately along with delay compensation as per the MNREGA Act.

Enclosures: MNREGA Job Card, bank passbook copy, muster roll copy

Yours faithfully,
[Name]
[Address]
[Date]
[Mobile Number]

problem_description: An employee is denied access to their Provident Fund contributions by their employer, or the employer has not deposited PF contributions despite deducting them from salaries.
workflow_steps: Step 1 – Check your EPF balance on the EPFO Member Portal using your UAN number.
Step 2 – Verify if employer has been regularly depositing PF contributions.
Step 3 – If contributions are missing, send a written complaint to your employer.
Step 4 – File a complaint with the Regional Provident Fund Commissioner (RPFC) office.
Step 5 – File an online complaint on the EPF Grievance Portal (epfigms.gov.in).
Step 6 – RPFC will initiate action against the employer under the EPF and MP Act.
Step 7 – Approach the Labour Court if the employer continues to evade.
required_documents: EPF passbook or statement showing non-deposit
escalation_path: RPFC Office → Labour Commissioner → Labour Court → High Court
complaint_template:
To,
The Regional Provident Fund Commissioner,
[City / Region]

Subject: Complaint regarding non-deposit of Provident Fund by employer

Respected Sir/Madam,

I, [Your Name], am employed at [Company Name] and my UAN is [UAN Number]. I have noticed that despite PF deductions from my salary, the employer has not deposited the contributions for [Months] in my EPF account.

I request your office to investigate and direct the employer to immediately deposit the due PF contributions with penalty.

Enclosures: Salary slips, EPF statement, UAN card, appointment letter

Yours faithfully,
[Name]
[Address]
[Date]
[Mobile Number]

problem_description: A citizen has witnessed a child below the legal working age being employed in a factory, shop, household, or other workplace, which is illegal under the Child Labour (Prohibition and Regulation) Act.
workflow_steps: Step 1 – Note the details of the child, employer, and location discreetly.
Step 2 – Call the Childline helpline (1098) to report immediately.
Step 3 – File a written complaint with the local police station.
Step 4 – Report to the District Child Labour Inspector under the Labour Department.
Step 5 – The Child Labour Inspector has the power to inspect premises and rescue the child.
Step 6 – File a complaint with the Child Welfare Committee (CWC) for the child's rehabilitation.
Step 7 – Report to the District Collector who is the District Magistrate responsible for child welfare.
Step 8 – Contact NGOs active in child rights if official response is slow.
required_documents: Photos or videos of child working (if safely possible)
Written complaint with details of employer and location
escalation_path: Childline (1098) → Police → Child Labour Inspector → Child Welfare Committee → District Collector
complaint_template:
To,
The District Child Labour Inspector,
[District]

Subject: Complaint regarding employment of child labour

Respected Sir/Madam,

I wish to report that a child aged approximately [Age] years is being employed as [Type of Work] at [Employer Name and Address]. This is a clear violation of the Child Labour (Prohibition and Regulation) Act, 1986.

I request your office to immediately inspect the premises, rescue the child, and take legal action against the employer.

I wish to remain anonymous but my contact number is [Mobile] if required.

Yours faithfully,
[Complainant / Anonymous]
[Date]

problem_description: A female employee is denied maternity leave, maternity pay, or other maternity benefits by her employer as entitled under the Maternity Benefit Act, 1961.
workflow_steps: Step 1 – Collect your pregnancy documentation (doctor's certificate, leave application submitted to employer).
Step 2 – Confirm your entitlement under the Maternity Benefit Act (26 weeks for 1st and 2nd child).
Step 3 – Send a written reminder to your employer citing the Maternity Benefit Act.
Step 4 – File a complaint with the Labour Commissioner if employer denies benefits.
Step 5 – The Labour Commissioner will conduct inquiry and direct the employer to provide benefits.
Step 6 – File a case in Labour Court if the employer does not comply.
Step 7 – Contact National Commission for Women or State Women's Commission for additional support.
escalation_path: Labour Commissioner → Labour Court → State Women's Commission → National Commission for Women
complaint_template:
To,
The Labour Commissioner,
[District]

Subject: Complaint regarding denial of maternity benefits

Respected Sir/Madam,

I, [Your Name], am employed at [Company Name] as [Designation]. I applied for maternity leave on [Date] with supporting medical documents. However, my employer has denied my maternity leave and maternity pay, which is in violation of the Maternity Benefit Act, 1961.

I am entitled to 26 weeks of paid maternity leave under the Act. I request your office to intervene and direct my employer to grant my legal maternity benefits.

Enclosures: Medical certificate, appointment letter, leave application, salary slips

Yours faithfully,
[Name]
[Address]
[Date]
[Mobile Number]

problem_description: A female employee faces sexual harassment at the workplace, including verbal, physical, or online harassment from a colleague, superior, or employer.
workflow_steps: Step 1 – Document all incidents of harassment with dates, times, places, and witnesses.
Step 2 – Save all evidence: messages, emails, witness statements.
Step 3 – File a complaint with the Internal Complaints Committee (ICC) of your organization within 3 months of the incident.
Step 4 – If no ICC exists or the employer is unresponsive, file a complaint with the Local Complaints Committee (LCC) at the Sub-Divisional level.
Step 5 – The ICC/LCC will conduct an inquiry and submit a report within 60 days.
Step 6 – File a police complaint under Section 354A IPC if criminal action is required.
Step 7 – Approach State Women's Commission or National Commission for Women if institutional response is inadequate.
Step 8 – File a civil lawsuit for damages if desired.
escalation_path: Internal Complaints Committee → Local Complaints Committee → Police → State Women's Commission → High Court
complaint_template:
To,
The Chairperson,
Internal Complaints Committee,
[Company Name]

Subject: Complaint of sexual harassment at workplace

Respected Madam/Sir,

I, [Your Name], working as [Designation] in [Department], wish to file a formal complaint of sexual harassment against [Respondent Name], [Designation].

On [Date(s)], the respondent [describe the incident clearly without unnecessary detail]. This conduct is unwelcome, offensive, and constitutes sexual harassment under the POSH Act, 2013.

I request the committee to conduct a prompt and fair inquiry into this complaint and take appropriate action.

Yours faithfully,
[Name]
[Date]
[Contact]

problem_description: A worker is being kept in bonded labour, forced to work without wages to repay a debt, or is prevented from leaving their employment through coercion or threats.
workflow_steps: Step 1 – If safe to do so, gather evidence of bondage: debt agreements, lack of freedom of movement, threats.
Step 2 – Contact the Childline (1098) if a minor is involved, or Police Emergency (112) if immediate threat exists.
Step 3 – File a complaint with the Sub-Divisional Magistrate (SDM) who has powers to release bonded labourers.
Step 4 – The SDM will conduct an inquiry and issue a release certificate under the Bonded Labour System (Abolition) Act.
Step 5 – File a police FIR for criminal offences under the Bonded Labour Act.
Step 6 – The released labourer is entitled to rehabilitation assistance from the state government.
Step 7 – Contact the District Labour Commissioner for formal rehabilitation support.
Step 8 – NGOs like Bachpan Bachao Andolan can assist with rescue and rehabilitation.
required_documents: Any debt bond or agreement (if obtainable)
Evidence of coercion or restriction of movement
escalation_path: Police Emergency (112) → SDM → District Collector → State Labour Department → National Human Rights Commission
complaint_template:
To,
The Sub-Divisional Magistrate,
[Sub-Division], [District]

Subject: Complaint regarding bonded labour

Respected Sir/Madam,

I wish to report that [Name of Victim] from [Village/Address] is being kept in bonded labour by [Employer Name] at [Location]. The victim is being forced to work without proper wages to repay a debt and is not allowed to leave freely.

This is a violation of the Bonded Labour System (Abolition) Act, 1976. I request your urgent intervention to rescue and release the victim and take legal action against the employer.

Yours faithfully,
[Name / NGO Representative]
[Date]
[Contact]

subcategory: Domestic Abuse / Physical Violence by Spouse
problem_description: A woman is facing physical violence from her husband or in-laws at home. She requires immediate protection, shelter, and legal action against the abuser.
workflow_steps: Step 1 – If in immediate danger, call the Women Helpline (1091) or Emergency Services (112).
Step 2 – Seek medical attention for injuries and ensure a medical examination report (Medico-Legal Case report) is prepared.
Step 3 – Approach the nearest Police Station and file an FIR under Section 498A IPC (cruelty by husband or relatives) and under the Protection of Women from Domestic Violence Act, 2005.
Step 4 – Contact the Protection Officer under the DV Act at the District Women and Child Development office.
Step 5 – The Protection Officer will help you file a Domestic Incident Report (DIR).
Step 6 – Apply through the Magistrate Court for a Protection Order, Residence Order, and Monetary Relief under the DV Act.
Step 7 – Apply for emergency shelter at a government shelter home or One Stop Centre.
Step 8 – Approach DLSA for free legal aid and representation.
required_documents: Any written threats or abusive messages
escalation_path: Women Helpline (1091) → Police FIR → Protection Officer → Magistrate Court → Sessions Court
complaint_template:
To,
The Station House Officer,
[Police Station Name]

Subject: Complaint of domestic violence under Protection of Women from Domestic Violence Act, 2005

Respected Sir/Madam,

I, [Your Name], residing at [Your Address], wish to file a complaint against my husband [Name] and/or in-laws [Names], residing at [Address].

I have been subjected to physical violence including [describe incidents briefly] on [Date(s)]. I have medical evidence of my injuries.

I request you to register an FIR and take immediate legal action. I also request police protection for my safety.

Enclosures: Medical report, photographs, identity proof

Yours faithfully,
[Name]
[Date]
[Mobile Number]
online_portals: https://wcddel.in (Delhi WCD – similar portals exist in all states)

problem_description: A woman or her family is being harassed, abused, or threatened by the husband or in-laws for additional dowry after marriage.
workflow_steps: Step 1 – Document all harassment incidents, demands, and threats with dates and witnesses.
Step 2 – Save all written or electronic communications containing dowry demands.
Step 3 – File an FIR at the local police station under Section 498A IPC (dowry harassment) and Section 4 of the Dowry Prohibition Act.
Step 4 – Contact the Protection Officer under the DV Act for immediate protection.
Step 5 – Apply in Magistrate Court for a Protection Order under the Domestic Violence Act.
Step 6 – File a separate complaint with the Dowry Prohibition Officer (a government-designated official).
Step 7 – Approach the Women's Commission for additional support and monitoring.
Step 8 – Seek free legal aid from DLSA for court proceedings.
required_documents: List of dowry items given at marriage
Screenshots of messages with dowry demands
Medical reports if physical violence occurred
escalation_path: Police FIR → Protection Officer → Magistrate Court → Sessions Court → State Women's Commission
complaint_template:
To,
The Station House Officer,
[Police Station Name]

Subject: FIR complaint regarding dowry harassment

Respected Sir/Madam,

I, [Your Name], am the wife of [Husband's Name] married on [Date]. Since our marriage, my husband and his family have been continuously demanding additional dowry of [describe demands]. When I was unable to fulfil these demands, I was subjected to harassment, mental torture, and physical abuse.

I request you to register an FIR under Section 498A IPC and Section 4 of the Dowry Prohibition Act and take action against my husband [Name] and in-laws [Names].

Enclosures: Marriage certificate, dowry list, screenshots of messages, medical report

Yours faithfully,
[Name]
[Date]
[Mobile Number]

problem_description: Parents going through separation or divorce dispute who will have custody of their minor children. One parent may be withholding children from the other or violating court-ordered custody terms.
workflow_steps: Step 1 – Consult a family lawyer or DLSA for understanding your rights under the Guardian and Wards Act.
Step 2 – File a petition in the Family Court for custody of the child.
Step 3 – The Family Court will evaluate the best interests of the child and may appoint a guardian.
Step 4 – If the other parent has illegally taken the child, file a Habeas Corpus petition in the High Court.
Step 5 – Submit evidence of your ability to provide care, education, and stability for the child.
Step 6 – Attend all court hearings and cooperate with the Child Welfare Officer or counsellor appointed by the court.
Step 7 – Mediation through the Family Court may be attempted before a final order.
required_documents: Evidence of child's welfare (school records, medical records)
Any existing court order regarding custody
escalation_path: Family Court → High Court → Supreme Court (in extreme cases)
complaint_template:
To,
The Principal Judge,
Family Court,
[City / District]

Subject: Petition for custody of minor child

Respected Sir/Madam,

I, [Your Name], am the [mother/father] of [Child's Name] born on [Date]. I wish to file a petition for custody of my minor child following my separation from my spouse [Spouse's Name].

I am capable of providing proper care, education, and a stable environment for my child. I request the court to grant custody of [Child's Name] to me in the best interests of the child.

Enclosures: Birth certificate, marriage certificate, income documents, identity proof

Yours faithfully,
[Name]
[Date]

problem_description: A wife, child, or dependent parent is not receiving maintenance or alimony as ordered by a court or as owed under personal law by a husband or family member.
workflow_steps: Step 1 – Collect evidence that maintenance is due and has not been paid: bank records, court order.
Step 2 – File a petition under Section 125 of the Criminal Procedure Code (CrPC) in the Magistrate Court for maintenance.
Step 3 – Alternatively, file for maintenance under personal law (Hindu Marriage Act, Muslim Women's Protection Act, etc.).
Step 4 – If a court order already exists and is not being followed, file an execution petition in the court that passed the order.
Step 5 – The court can direct attachment of the defaulter's property or salary for recovery of maintenance.
Step 6 – Contact DLSA for free legal representation.
Step 7 – Approach the Family Court Welfare Officer for mediation and assistance.
required_documents: Proof of income of the person required to pay maintenance
Bank statements showing non-receipt of maintenance
escalation_path: Magistrate Court → Family Court → High Court
complaint_template:
To,
The Judicial Magistrate,
[District]

Subject: Application for maintenance under Section 125 CrPC

Respected Sir/Madam,

I, [Your Name], am the wife/child/dependent of [Respondent Name]. I am unable to maintain myself due to [describe circumstances]. The respondent has the means to provide maintenance but has refused to do so.

I request the court to direct the respondent to pay monthly maintenance of Rs. [Amount] as per law.

Enclosures: Marriage certificate, income evidence of respondent, identity proof

Yours faithfully,
[Name]
[Date]

problem_description: A minor girl or woman is being forced into marriage against her will, or a marriage is being arranged for a child below 18 years (girl) or 21 years (boy) in violation of the Prohibition of Child Marriage Act.
workflow_steps: Step 1 – Call the Childline helpline (1098) immediately if a child is at risk of being married.
Step 2 – Call the Women Helpline (1091) or Police Emergency (112) to prevent a forced marriage.
Step 3 – File a complaint with the local police station about the planned child or forced marriage.
Step 4 – Report to the Child Marriage Prohibition Officer (CMPO) in your district.
Step 5 – The CMPO or District Collector can issue an injunction to prevent the marriage from happening.
Step 6 – A child marriage already performed is voidable – the minor can apply to the court to void it.
Step 7 – Approach the District Legal Services Authority for legal representation.
required_documents: Age proof of the minor (birth certificate, school certificate)
Information about when and where the marriage is planned
escalation_path: Childline (1098) → Police → Child Marriage Prohibition Officer → District Collector → Magistrate Court
complaint_template:
To,
The Child Marriage Prohibition Officer,
[District]

Subject: Complaint regarding planned child/forced marriage

Respected Sir/Madam,

I wish to report that a girl/boy named [Name], aged approximately [Age] years, residing at [Address] is being forced into marriage on [Date]. This is in violation of the Prohibition of Child Marriage Act, 2006.

I request your immediate intervention to prevent this marriage and protect the minor/person from this forced situation.

Yours faithfully,
[Name / Anonymous]
[Date]
[Contact]
online_portals: State Women and Child Development Portal

subcategory: Stalking and Harassment by Family Member or Ex-Partner
problem_description: A woman is being stalked, followed, threatened, or harassed by a family member, ex-partner, or spouse who repeatedly contacts her without consent.
workflow_steps: Step 1 – Document all incidents of stalking with dates, locations, and evidence.
Step 2 – Save all harassing messages, calls, and digital evidence.
Step 3 – File a police complaint under Section 354D IPC (stalking) at the local police station.
Step 4 – Apply for a restraining order from the Magistrate Court.
Step 5 – File a complaint under the DV Act with the Protection Officer if the stalker is a domestic partner.
Step 6 – Contact the Women Helpline (1091) for immediate assistance and counselling.
Step 7 – Approach the State Women's Commission if police are unresponsive.
required_documents: Evidence of stalking: screenshots, call records
Written account of incidents with dates
escalation_path: Police Station → Magistrate Court → Sessions Court → State Women's Commission
complaint_template:
To,
The Station House Officer,
[Police Station Name]

Subject: Complaint regarding stalking and harassment

Respected Sir/Madam,

I, [Your Name], wish to file a complaint against [Stalker's Name] who has been stalking and harassing me since [Date]. The incidents include [describe briefly: following, repeated calls, messages, appearing at my residence/workplace].

This conduct is causing me fear and psychological distress. I request police action under Section 354D IPC and protection from further harassment.

Enclosures: Screenshots of messages, call records, written incident account

Yours faithfully,
[Name]
[Date]
[Mobile Number]

problem_description: A spouse has abandoned their partner and children without financial support, leaving the family in financial distress.
workflow_steps: Step 1 – Document the date of abandonment and gather evidence of desertion.
Step 2 – File a petition for maintenance under Section 125 CrPC in Magistrate Court.
Step 3 – File a petition for divorce on grounds of desertion in Family Court if desired (2 years of desertion required under Hindu Marriage Act).
Step 4 – Apply for interim maintenance for yourself and children during pending proceedings.
Step 5 – Report to the local police if the desertion involved domestic violence or threats.
Step 6 – Approach DLSA for free legal aid and representation.
Step 7 – Contact One Stop Centre (181) for counselling and support services.
escalation_path: Magistrate Court (Maintenance) → Family Court (Divorce/Custody) → High Court
complaint_template:
To,
The Judicial Magistrate,
[District]

Subject: Application for maintenance due to desertion by spouse

Respected Sir/Madam,

I, [Your Name], am the wife of [Husband's Name]. My husband abandoned me and our [Number] children on [Date] and has not provided any financial support since then. I am unable to maintain myself and my children without support.

I request the court to direct my husband to pay monthly maintenance of Rs. [Amount] under Section 125 CrPC.

Enclosures: Marriage certificate, identity proof, children's birth certificates

Yours faithfully,
[Name]
[Date]

problem_description: Money has been fraudulently debited from a citizen's bank account through a UPI payment made under deception, threat, or by a scammer posing as a bank or service provider.
workflow_steps: Step 1 – Immediately block your UPI app and contact your bank to freeze any further transactions.
Step 2 – Take screenshots of the fraudulent transaction details.
Step 3 – Call the Cyber Fraud Helpline (1930) and report the fraud immediately.
Step 4 – File an online complaint on the National Cyber Crime Reporting Portal (cybercrime.gov.in).
Step 5 – Visit the nearest Cyber Crime Police Station or file an FIR at the local police station.
Step 6 – Provide all transaction IDs, phone numbers used by the fraudster, and screenshots.
Step 7 – Contact your bank to file a formal chargeback request for the fraudulent transaction.
Step 8 – If money is not recovered, approach the Banking Ombudsman for a banking complaint.
required_documents: Phone number or UPI ID of fraudster
Copy of communication with fraudster (messages, calls)

authorities.name: Reserve Bank of India (if banking issue)

escalation_path: Cyber Fraud Helpline (1930) → Cyber Crime Portal → Cyber Crime Police → Banking Ombudsman → RBI
complaint_template:
To,
The Station House Officer,
Cyber Crime Police Station / [Police Station Name]

Subject: FIR complaint regarding UPI payment fraud

Respected Sir/Madam,

I, [Your Name], wish to report that on [Date] at approximately [Time], an amount of Rs. [Amount] was fraudulently transferred from my bank account (Account No. [Partial Account No.]) through UPI transaction ID [Transaction ID].

The fraud was committed by [describe how: fake customer care call / QR code scam / OTP sharing]. I was deceived into authorizing the payment.

I request immediate investigation, freezing of the beneficiary account, and recovery of the fraudulent amount.

Enclosures: Transaction screenshot, bank statement, identity proof

Yours faithfully,
[Name]
[Address]
[Date]
[Mobile Number]

problem_description: A scammer called the victim posing as a bank representative or government official and obtained the One-Time Password (OTP), leading to unauthorized account access or money transfer.
workflow_steps: Step 1 – Immediately call your bank to block your account and reverse unauthorized transactions.
Step 2 – Call the Cyber Fraud Helpline (1930) to report the fraud and attempt to freeze the beneficiary account.
Step 3 – Take note of the fraudster's phone number and all details of the conversation.
Step 4 – File a complaint on the National Cyber Crime Reporting Portal (cybercrime.gov.in).
Step 5 – Visit the Cyber Crime Police Station or local police station to file an FIR.
Step 6 – File a banking fraud complaint with your bank's branch manager and escalate to Banking Ombudsman.
Step 7 – File a complaint with TRAI (Telecom Regulatory Authority of India) about fraudulent SIM use.
required_documents: Call records / call duration logs from your phone
escalation_path: Cyber Fraud Helpline → Cyber Crime Portal → Police FIR → Banking Ombudsman → RBI
complaint_template:
To,
The Station House Officer,
Cyber Crime Police Station

Subject: Complaint regarding OTP fraud and unauthorized bank transaction

Respected Sir/Madam,

I, [Your Name], received a phone call on [Date] from [Phone Number] from a person claiming to be a [bank/government] representative. The caller convinced me to share my OTP, following which Rs. [Amount] was debited from my account without my authorization.

I request immediate investigation and recovery of the fraudulent amount.

Enclosures: Bank statement, SMS screenshots, call record, identity proof

Yours faithfully,
[Name]
[Date]
[Mobile Number]

problem_description: A customer paid for goods through an online shopping platform or social media seller but never received the product, received a fake or damaged product, or the seller vanished after payment.
workflow_steps: Step 1 – Collect all evidence: order confirmation, payment receipt, seller's profile, communication.
Step 2 – File a complaint with the online shopping platform's customer service and request a refund.
Step 3 – If the platform does not respond, file a complaint on the National Consumer Helpline (1915) or consumerhelpline.gov.in.
Step 4 – File a complaint on the National Cyber Crime Reporting Portal if it involves fraud.
Step 5 – File a case in the Consumer Disputes Redressal Commission for refund and compensation.
Step 6 – File an FIR at the police station if the amount is significant or fraud is deliberate.
Step 7 – Report the seller's social media account or listing for fraudulent activity.

authorities.name: Local Police Station / Cyber Crime Police

escalation_path: Platform Customer Service → National Consumer Helpline → Consumer Commission → Police FIR → Cyber Crime Police
complaint_template:
To,
The District Consumer Disputes Redressal Commission,
[District]

Subject: Consumer complaint regarding online shopping fraud

Respected Sir/Madam,

I, [Your Name], placed an order for [Product Name] on [Platform/Seller Name] on [Date] and paid Rs. [Amount] via [payment method]. Despite payment, I have not received the product / received a defective/fake product, and the seller is unresponsive.

This is a clear case of unfair trade practice and consumer fraud. I request the commission to direct the seller to refund Rs. [Amount] along with compensation and litigation costs.

Enclosures: Order confirmation, payment receipt, communication records

Yours faithfully,
[Name]
[Date]
[Mobile Number]

problem_description: A fraudster has created a fake social media profile impersonating the victim, used it to cheat the victim's contacts, or a scammer is using a fake identity to defraud people.
workflow_steps: Step 1 – Take screenshots of the fake profile and all fraudulent communications.
Step 2 – Report the fake profile directly to the social media platform.
Step 3 – File a complaint on the National Cyber Crime Reporting Portal (cybercrime.gov.in).
Step 4 – File an FIR at the Cyber Crime Police Station for identity theft and impersonation.
Step 5 – Inform your friends and family about the fake profile to prevent further fraud.
Step 6 – Request the platform to take down the fake account immediately.
Step 7 – Contact the Cyber Crime Police with the IP address request if known.
required_documents: Screenshots of fraudulent messages sent through fake profile
Evidence that the profile is fake (your own genuine profile)
escalation_path: Cyber Crime Portal → Cyber Crime Police Station → State Cyber Cell → CERT-In
complaint_template:
To,
The Station House Officer,
Cyber Crime Police Station

Subject: Complaint regarding social media impersonation/identity theft

Respected Sir/Madam,

I, [Your Name], wish to report that an unknown person has created a fake profile on [Platform Name] using my name, photographs, and personal information. This fake profile is being used to deceive my contacts and extract money.

I request immediate investigation, removal of the fake profile, and legal action against the impersonator.

Enclosures: Screenshots of fake profile, identity proof, screenshots of fraudulent messages

Yours faithfully,
[Name]
[Date]
[Mobile Number]

subcategory: Job Offer Fraud / Fake Recruitment Scam
problem_description: A person paid money to a fraudulent recruitment agency or individual promising a job that never materialized, or received a fake job offer letter demanding payment for placement.
workflow_steps: Step 1 – Collect all evidence: fake job offer letter, payment receipts, communication with fraudster.
Step 2 – Verify whether the company mentioned in the offer actually exists.
Step 3 – File a complaint at the local police station.
Step 4 – File a complaint on the National Cyber Crime Reporting Portal if fraud occurred online.
Step 5 – Report to the Ministry of Labour about the fraudulent recruitment agency.
Step 6 – File a consumer complaint for recovery of fraudulent fee paid.
Step 7 – Report to the Labour Commissioner if a registered agency is involved.
escalation_path: Police FIR → Cyber Crime Portal → Labour Commissioner → Consumer Forum
complaint_template:
To,
The Station House Officer,
[Police Station Name]

Subject: Complaint regarding job offer fraud

Respected Sir/Madam,

I, [Your Name], was approached by [Name/Agency] with a job offer from [Fake Company Name]. I paid Rs. [Amount] as placement/processing fee on [Date]. After payment, the agent became unreachable and no job was provided.

This is a clear fraud and I request an FIR to be registered and my money to be recovered.

Enclosures: Fake offer letter, payment receipt, communication, bank statement

Yours faithfully,
[Name]
[Date]
[Mobile Number]

problem_description: A citizen receives a fraudulent message claiming they have won a lottery, prize money, or lucky draw, and is asked to pay taxes or processing fees to claim the fake prize.
workflow_steps: Step 1 – Do NOT pay any amount claimed as tax or processing fee.
Step 2 – Save all messages, emails, and communication from the fraudster.
Step 3 – Report the fraudulent message to Cyber Crime Helpline (1930).
Step 4 – File a complaint on the National Cyber Crime Reporting Portal.
Step 5 – File a police complaint if any money was already paid.
Step 6 – Warn family members and friends about the scam.
required_documents: Payment receipts if money was paid
Phone number or email of fraudster
escalation_path: Cyber Fraud Helpline → Cyber Crime Portal → Police FIR
complaint_template:
To,
The Station House Officer,
Cyber Crime Police Station

Subject: Complaint regarding lottery/prize fraud

Respected Sir/Madam,

I, [Your Name], received a message/call on [Date] claiming I had won a lottery prize of Rs. [Amount]. I was asked to pay Rs. [Amount] as processing/tax fee. I paid Rs. [Amount] but no prize was delivered and the fraudster is now unreachable.

I request investigation and recovery of my money.

Enclosures: Messages/emails from fraudster, payment proof, identity proof

Yours faithfully,
[Name]
[Date]
[Mobile Number]

problem_description: A scammer has misused the victim's Aadhaar number, PAN, or KYC details to open fake bank accounts, take loans, or commit financial fraud in the victim's name.
workflow_steps: Step 1 – Check your credit report (CIBIL) to identify any unauthorized loans or accounts.
Step 2 – Lock your Aadhaar biometric data on the UIDAI website (uidai.gov.in) or mAadhaar app.
Step 3 – File a complaint with UIDAI on the Aadhaar helpline (1947) or website.
Step 4 – File an FIR at the Cyber Crime Police Station.
Step 5 – File a complaint on the National Cyber Crime Reporting Portal.
Step 6 – Contact banks where fraudulent accounts were opened to report and close them.
Step 7 – File a complaint with the Banking Ombudsman if banks are unresponsive.
required_documents: CIBIL / Credit report showing fraudulent accounts
escalation_path: UIDAI Complaint → Cyber Crime Police → Banking Ombudsman → RBI
complaint_template:
To,
The Station House Officer,
Cyber Crime Police Station

Subject: Complaint regarding misuse of Aadhaar / KYC details

Respected Sir/Madam,

I, [Your Name], have discovered that my Aadhaar number [XXXX-XXXX-XXXX] and KYC documents have been misused to [open a bank account/take a loan/commit fraud] without my knowledge or consent.

I request immediate investigation, cancellation of fraudulent accounts/loans, and legal action against the perpetrators.

Enclosures: Aadhaar copy, CIBIL report, evidence of fraud

Yours faithfully,
[Name]
[Date]
[Mobile Number]

problem_description: A consumer purchased an electronic appliance (TV, refrigerator, mobile phone, etc.) that turned out to be defective, and the seller or manufacturer refuses to repair, replace, or refund.
workflow_steps: Step 1 – Keep all purchase documents: bill, warranty card, packaging.
Step 2 – Report the defect to the seller and manufacturer in writing within the warranty period.
Step 3 – Send a legal notice to the seller and manufacturer by registered post.
Step 4 – Call the National Consumer Helpline (1915) for guidance and mediation.
Step 5 – File an online consumer complaint at consumerhelpline.gov.in.
Step 6 – File a case in the District Consumer Disputes Redressal Commission (DCDRC) for claims up to Rs. 50 lakhs.
Step 7 – Appeal to the State Consumer Commission for claims between Rs. 50 lakhs and Rs. 2 crores.
Step 8 – Appeal to the National Consumer Commission for claims above Rs. 2 crores.
escalation_path: Seller/Manufacturer → National Consumer Helpline → DCDRC → State Commission → National Commission
complaint_template:
To,
The President,
District Consumer Disputes Redressal Commission,
[District]

Subject: Consumer complaint regarding defective product

Respected Sir/Madam,

I, [Your Name], purchased a [Product Name / Brand] from [Seller Name], [Address] on [Date] for Rs. [Amount]. The product developed defects within the warranty period including [describe defects].

Despite written complaints and service centre visits, the seller/manufacturer has refused to repair, replace, or refund the product.

I request the commission to direct the opposite party to replace the product / refund Rs. [Amount] along with compensation of Rs. [Amount] for inconvenience.

Enclosures: Purchase bill, warranty card, complaint letters, service reports

Yours faithfully,
[Name]
[Date]
[Mobile Number]

problem_description: A consumer received substandard, expired, or counterfeit medicines from a pharmacy, or suffered harm due to a defective pharmaceutical product.
workflow_steps: Step 1 – Preserve the medicine packaging, batch number, and purchase receipt.
Step 2 – Report to the pharmacy and demand a replacement or refund.
Step 3 – File a complaint with the Drug Control Authority (Central or State).
Step 4 – Report to the Central Drugs Standard Control Organisation (CDSCO).
Step 5 – If physical harm occurred, seek medical treatment and preserve medical records.
Step 6 – File a complaint in the District Consumer Commission for compensation.
Step 7 – File an FIR if the medicines were deliberately counterfeit.
escalation_path: Pharmacy → State Drug Control → CDSCO → Consumer Commission → Police FIR
complaint_template:
To,
The Drug Control Authority,
[State]

Subject: Complaint regarding defective/counterfeit medicine

Respected Sir/Madam,

I, [Your Name], purchased [Medicine Name] (Batch No. [Batch No.], expiry date [Date]) from [Pharmacy Name] on [Date]. The medicine was found to be [expired/defective/counterfeit/caused adverse reaction].

I request your authority to investigate this matter, take action against the pharmacy, and arrange for testing of the batch.

Enclosures: Medicine packaging, purchase receipt, medical reports

Yours faithfully,
[Name]
[Date]
[Mobile Number]

problem_description: A farmer purchased defective agricultural equipment (tractor parts, pumps), spurious seeds, or ineffective pesticides that resulted in crop failure or financial loss.
workflow_steps: Step 1 – Keep purchase receipts, bills, and packaging for seeds, pesticides, or equipment.
Step 2 – Document the crop failure or equipment defect with photographs and expert assessment.
Step 3 – Report to the supplier and manufacturer in writing.
Step 4 – File a complaint with the District Agriculture Officer.
Step 5 – File a complaint with the State Agriculture Department's Quality Control Cell.
Step 6 – File a consumer case in the District Consumer Commission for compensation.
Step 7 – File an FIR if the products were fraudulently sold as genuine.
required_documents: Seed / pesticide packet with batch number
Photographs of crop failure or equipment defect
escalation_path: District Agriculture Officer → State Agriculture Department → Consumer Commission → Police FIR
complaint_template:
To,
The District Agriculture Officer,
[District]

Subject: Complaint regarding defective seeds/pesticides/agricultural equipment

Respected Sir/Madam,

I, [Your Name], a farmer in village [Village Name], purchased [Product Name] from [Supplier Name] on [Date] for Rs. [Amount]. After using the product, my crop [describe damage / equipment failure]. I suspect the product was spurious/substandard.

I request your office to inspect the matter, test the product quality, and take action against the supplier.

Enclosures: Purchase bill, product packaging, photographs of crop damage

Yours faithfully,
[Name]
[Date]
[Mobile Number]

problem_description: An insurance company has rejected a legitimate claim without valid reason, paid an insufficient amount, or delayed the claim settlement unreasonably.
workflow_steps: Step 1 – Collect the rejection letter and all insurance policy documents.
Step 2 – Review the policy terms to confirm your entitlement.
Step 3 – Send a written complaint to the insurance company's Grievance Officer.
Step 4 – If unresolved within 30 days, file a complaint with the Insurance Ombudsman.
Step 5 – File a consumer case in the District Consumer Commission.
Step 6 – File a complaint with the Insurance Regulatory and Development Authority of India (IRDAI).
Step 7 – Approach Civil Court as a last resort.
escalation_path: Insurance Company Grievance → Insurance Ombudsman → IRDAI → Consumer Commission → Civil Court
complaint_template:
To,
The Insurance Ombudsman,
[Region]

Subject: Complaint regarding unfair rejection of insurance claim

Respected Sir/Madam,

I, [Your Name], hold Policy No. [Policy Number] with [Insurance Company Name]. I filed a claim on [Date] for [reason: health treatment / accident / property damage] of Rs. [Amount]. My claim was rejected on [Date] citing [rejection reason] which I believe is incorrect and against the policy terms.

I request you to direct the insurance company to settle my legitimate claim of Rs. [Amount].

Enclosures: Policy document, claim form, rejection letter, supporting documents

Yours faithfully,
[Name]
[Date]
[Mobile Number]

subcategory: Bank Service Deficiency / Unauthorized Bank Charges
problem_description: A bank has levied unauthorized charges, failed to process a transaction correctly, frozen an account without reason, or provided deficient banking services causing financial loss.
workflow_steps: Step 1 – Collect your bank statements showing unauthorized charges or incorrect transactions.
Step 2 – File a written complaint with the Bank Branch Manager.
Step 3 – If unresolved within 30 days, escalate to the bank's internal Grievance Redressal Officer.
Step 4 – File a complaint with the Banking Ombudsman if the bank does not respond within 30 days of the internal complaint.
Step 5 – File a consumer case in the District Consumer Commission.
Step 6 – File a complaint with RBI if systemic issues are involved.
Step 7 – Approach the Civil Court for larger monetary claims.
escalation_path: Bank Branch → Bank Grievance Officer → Banking Ombudsman → RBI → Consumer Commission
complaint_template:
To,
The Banking Ombudsman,
[Region]

Subject: Complaint regarding unauthorized bank charges / deficient service

Respected Sir/Madam,

I, [Your Name], hold Account No. [Account Number] at [Bank Name], [Branch Name]. My bank has levied unauthorized charges of Rs. [Amount] on [Date(s)] / failed to process my transaction of Rs. [Amount] dated [Date] / frozen my account without reason.

Despite a written complaint to the Branch Manager on [Date], the issue remains unresolved.

I request you to direct the bank to refund the unauthorized charges / rectify the error.

Enclosures: Bank statement, complaint to bank, bank's response, identity proof

Yours faithfully,
[Name]
[Date]
[Mobile Number]

subcategory: Medical Negligence by Hospital or Doctor
problem_description: A patient or their family member suffered harm due to negligent treatment, wrong diagnosis, incorrect surgery, or substandard care by a hospital or medical professional.
workflow_steps: Step 1 – Collect all medical records, prescriptions, test reports, and bills.
Step 2 – Obtain an independent medical opinion on the negligence.
Step 3 – File a written complaint with the Hospital Administration.
Step 4 – File a complaint with the State Medical Council against the doctor.
Step 5 – File a consumer case in the District Consumer Commission (medical services are consumer services).
Step 6 – File a police complaint if criminal negligence is suspected.
Step 7 – Approach the National Human Rights Commission if fundamental rights are violated.
required_documents: Medical records (case sheets, prescriptions, test reports)
escalation_path: Hospital Administration → State Medical Council → Consumer Commission → Police FIR → NHRC
complaint_template:
To,
The President,
District Consumer Disputes Redressal Commission,
[District]

Subject: Consumer complaint regarding medical negligence

Respected Sir/Madam,

I, [Your Name], am filing this complaint on behalf of [Patient Name] who was treated at [Hospital Name] from [Date] to [Date]. Due to the negligence of Dr. [Doctor Name] in [describe negligence: wrong diagnosis / incorrect treatment / delayed care], the patient suffered [describe harm / death].

This constitutes deficiency in service under the Consumer Protection Act. I request compensation of Rs. [Amount] for medical expenses, suffering, and loss.

Enclosures: Medical records, hospital bills, independent medical opinion

Yours faithfully,
[Name]
[Date]
[Mobile Number]

problem_description: A consumer receives an excessively high electricity bill, is charged for units not consumed, or the electricity department refuses to correct a billing error.
workflow_steps: Step 1 – Keep the disputed electricity bill and your meter reading records.
Step 2 – File a written complaint with the electricity department's local office.
Step 3 – Request a meter test (meter inspection) in writing.
Step 4 – If unresolved, approach the Consumer Grievance Redressal Forum (CGRF) of the electricity company.
Step 5 – File a complaint with the State Electricity Regulatory Commission (SERC).
Step 6 – File a consumer case in the District Consumer Commission.
Step 7 – Approach the Electricity Ombudsman for further appeal.
escalation_path: Electricity Department → CGRF → Electricity Ombudsman → SERC → Consumer Commission
complaint_template:
To,
The Consumer Grievance Redressal Forum,
[Electricity Company Name]

Subject: Complaint regarding inflated electricity bill

Respected Sir/Madam,

I, [Your Name], am a consumer with meter no. [Meter No.] at [Address]. My electricity bill for [Month] was Rs. [Amount], which is [X times] higher than my usual consumption. My actual meter reading on [Date] was [Reading Units].

I believe this is a billing error. I request you to investigate, correct the bill, and refund the excess amount charged.

Enclosures: Disputed bill, previous bills, meter reading record, identity proof

Yours faithfully,
[Name]
[Date]
[Mobile Number]
helplines: Electricity Department Helpline: (State specific – e.g., MSEDCL: 19122)

problem_description: A mobile or internet service provider is providing poor network coverage, charging for services not availed, not resolving complaints, or disconnecting service unfairly.
workflow_steps: Step 1 – File a complaint with your telecom operator's customer care.
Step 2 – If unresolved within 30 days, escalate to the telecom company's Nodal Officer.
Step 3 – If still unresolved within 90 days, file a complaint with the Telecom Regulatory Authority of India (TRAI).
Step 4 – File a complaint on the TRAI Consumer Complaint Portal.
Step 5 – File a consumer case in the District Consumer Commission.
Step 6 – Contact the Department of Telecommunications (DoT) for serious issues.
escalation_path: Telecom Customer Care → Nodal Officer → TRAI → Consumer Commission
complaint_template:
To,
The Nodal Officer,
[Telecom Company Name]

Subject: Complaint regarding deficient telecom service

Respected Sir/Madam,

I, [Your Name], am a subscriber of [Telecom Company] with mobile number [Mobile No.]. I have been experiencing [poor network / incorrect billing / unauthorized charges / service disconnection] since [Date].

Despite calling customer care on [Complaint Reference No.] on [Date], the issue remains unresolved.

I request immediate rectification and refund of Rs. [Amount] charged incorrectly.

Yours faithfully,
[Name]
[Date]
[Mobile Number]

problem_description: A citizen's property, vehicle, or valuables have been stolen and they wish to file a police complaint to recover property and pursue legal action against the thief.
workflow_steps: Step 1 – Note all details about the stolen property: description, serial numbers, value, photographs if available.
Step 2 – Visit the local police station immediately and file an FIR under Section 379 IPC.
Step 3 – Provide all details of the theft including date, time, location, and any witnesses.
Step 4 – Obtain a copy of the FIR – this is your legal right.
Step 5 – If the police refuse to register the FIR, file a complaint with the Superintendent of Police (SP).
Step 6 – Use the FIR copy to file insurance claims for stolen property.
Step 7 – Follow up regularly with the investigating officer for updates on the case.
Step 8 – Approach the Magistrate Court if police are non-cooperative.
required_documents: List of stolen property with descriptions and values
Bank account details for cash stolen from account
escalation_path: Local Police Station → Superintendent of Police → Magistrate Court → High Court
complaint_template:
To,
The Station House Officer,
[Police Station Name]

Subject: FIR complaint regarding theft

Respected Sir/Madam,

I, [Your Name], residing at [Address], wish to report that on [Date] at approximately [Time], property worth Rs. [Amount] was stolen from [Location]. The stolen items include [list items with descriptions and serial numbers].

I request you to register an FIR and investigate the theft at the earliest.

Enclosures: List of stolen items, photographs, identity proof

Yours faithfully,
[Name]
[Date]
[Mobile Number]

problem_description: A citizen has been physically assaulted, beaten, or attacked by another person and needs to file a criminal complaint and seek medical attention.
workflow_steps: Step 1 – Seek immediate medical treatment and ensure a Medico-Legal Case (MLC) report is prepared by the doctor.
Step 2 – Visit the local police station and file an FIR under Section 351/352 IPC (assault) or Section 323/325 IPC (causing hurt).
Step 3 – Provide names of the assailant(s) and any witnesses.
Step 4 – Obtain a copy of the FIR.
Step 5 – If the assault was severe, also file a complaint under Section 307 IPC (attempt to murder) if life was threatened.
Step 6 – If the police are non-responsive, approach the Superintendent of Police or Magistrate.
Step 7 – Consult a lawyer about filing a private complaint in Magistrate Court if needed.
required_documents: Names and contact details of witnesses
escalation_path: Police Station → SP → Magistrate Court → Sessions Court
complaint_template:
To,
The Station House Officer,
[Police Station Name]

Subject: FIR complaint regarding physical assault

Respected Sir/Madam,

I, [Your Name], residing at [Address], wish to report that on [Date] at approximately [Time], I was physically assaulted by [Assailant Name(s)] at [Location]. The assault caused me [describe injuries]. I have obtained medical treatment and an MLC report.

I request you to register an FIR and take appropriate legal action against the assailant(s).

Enclosures: MLC report, photographs, identity proof

Yours faithfully,
[Name]
[Date]
[Mobile Number]

problem_description: A citizen is being continuously harassed, intimidated, or threatened by a neighbour or local person, disrupting their peaceful living.
workflow_steps: Step 1 – Document all incidents of harassment with dates, times, and details.
Step 2 – Try to resolve the matter through local mediation (Gram Panchayat, neighbourhood committee) first.
Step 3 – File a written complaint with the local police station.
Step 4 – Request police to issue a warning or notice to the harassing party.
Step 5 – Apply to the Magistrate Court for a restraining order or peace bond if harassment continues.
Step 6 – If caste-based or communal harassment is involved, file under SC/ST Atrocities Act.
Step 7 – Approach the Superintendent of Police if local police are non-responsive.
required_documents: Written account of harassment incidents with dates
escalation_path: Gram Panchayat → Police Station → SP → Magistrate Court
complaint_template:
To,
The Station House Officer,
[Police Station Name]

Subject: Complaint regarding persistent harassment by neighbour

Respected Sir/Madam,

I, [Your Name], residing at [Address], wish to report that my neighbour [Name] residing at [Address] has been continuously harassing me since [Date] by [describe harassment: verbal abuse / threats / property damage / obstruction].

Despite requests to stop, the harassment continues. I request police intervention and appropriate action to ensure my peaceful living.

Enclosures: Written incident log, witness statements, identity proof

Yours faithfully,
[Name]
[Date]
[Mobile Number]

problem_description: A family member or person known to the citizen has gone missing and the family needs to file a complaint for search and rescue.
workflow_steps: Step 1 – File a missing person complaint at the local police station immediately. There is no waiting period required.
Step 2 – Provide a recent photograph, physical description, last known location, and clothing.
Step 3 – Request the police to register the case in the Crime and Criminal Tracking Network System (CCTNS).
Step 4 – If a child is missing, call the Childline (1098) and request an Amber Alert.
Step 5 – Contact the Missing Persons' Cell at the State Police Headquarters.
Step 6 – Check railway stations, bus stands, and hospitals with police assistance.
Step 7 – If trafficking is suspected, also file a complaint with the Anti-Human Trafficking Unit.
Step 8 – File a complaint with the National Crime Records Bureau (NCRB) for national-level search.
required_documents: Description of clothing on the day of disappearance
Contact details of people who last saw the person
escalation_path: Local Police → Missing Persons Cell → Anti-Trafficking Unit → State CID → NCRB
complaint_template:
To,
The Station House Officer,
[Police Station Name]

Subject: Missing person complaint

Respected Sir/Madam,

I, [Your Name], am the [relation] of [Missing Person's Name], aged [Age], residing at [Address]. [He/She] was last seen on [Date] at [Location] wearing [clothing description].

I am very concerned for [his/her] safety and request you to register a missing person complaint and initiate a search immediately.

Enclosures: Recent photograph, identity proof

Yours faithfully,
[Name]
[Date]
[Mobile Number]

subcategory: Police Refusing to File FIR (Non-Registration of Complaint)
problem_description: A citizen has gone to the police station to file an FIR but the police are refusing to register the complaint, which is a violation of the citizen's rights.
workflow_steps: Step 1 – Request the police officer to explain in writing why the FIR is being refused.
Step 2 – Send a written complaint about FIR refusal to the Superintendent of Police by registered post.
Step 3 – File a complaint with the State Police Complaints Authority.
Step 4 – File a complaint directly before the Magistrate Court under Section 156(3) CrPC – the magistrate can order the police to register the FIR.
Step 5 – File a writ petition in the High Court if all else fails.
Step 6 – Approach the National Human Rights Commission if rights are violated.
Step 7 – Contact the District Legal Services Authority for free legal assistance.
required_documents: Postal receipt if sent by registered post
escalation_path: Local Police → SP → State Police Complaints Authority → Magistrate Court → High Court → NHRC
complaint_template:
To,
The Superintendent of Police,
[District]

Subject: Complaint regarding refusal of police to register FIR

Respected Sir/Madam,

I, [Your Name], visited [Police Station Name] on [Date] to file an FIR regarding [brief description of crime]. The Station House Officer refused to register my FIR without giving a valid reason.

Refusing to register an FIR is a violation of Section 154 CrPC. I request you to direct the concerned police station to immediately register my complaint and take action.

Enclosures: Written application previously submitted to police, identity proof

Yours faithfully,
[Name]
[Date]
[Mobile Number]
online_portals: State Police Portal for Complaints against Police

problem_description: A citizen is being extorted for money through threats, blackmail, or intimidation – either by an individual, criminal gang, or someone who obtained compromising information.
workflow_steps: Step 1 – Do NOT pay the extortion demand.
Step 2 – Document all threats, messages, and demands with evidence.
Step 3 – File an FIR at the local police station under Section 383/384 IPC (extortion).
Step 4 – Report cyber blackmail to the Cyber Crime Helpline (1930) and Cyber Crime Portal.
Step 5 – Provide police with all contact details, messages, and evidence of the extortionist.
Step 6 – Request police to use technical surveillance to identify and catch the perpetrator.
Step 7 – Seek protection from the Magistrate Court if threats to life are involved.
required_documents: Evidence of payment if any was made under coercion
escalation_path: Local Police → SP → State Crime Branch → Magistrate Court
complaint_template:
To,
The Station House Officer,
[Police Station Name]

Subject: FIR complaint regarding extortion/blackmail

Respected Sir/Madam,

I, [Your Name], wish to report that I am being extorted by [Name/Unknown Person] who is demanding Rs. [Amount] under threat of [describe threat]. The threats were communicated through [phone/message/in person] on [Date(s)].

I request immediate action to arrest the extortionist and protect me from further threats.

Enclosures: Screenshots of messages, call records, identity proof

Yours faithfully,
[Name]
[Date]
[Mobile Number]

category: सरकारी योजना और सार्वजनिक सेवा समस्याएं
subcategory: Ration Card Issue / Denial of PDS Benefits
problem_description: A citizen has been denied their entitlement of ration (food grains) under the Public Distribution System (PDS), or there are issues with their ration card (wrong name, wrong category, or ration shop refusing to provide goods).
workflow_steps: Step 1 – Collect your ration card and check your entitlement (Above Poverty Line / Below Poverty Line / Antyodaya).
Step 2 – Visit the Fair Price Shop and note the refusal in writing or on the ePoS machine.
Step 3 – File a written complaint with the Circle Supply Inspector or District Supply Officer.
Step 4 – File a complaint on the State Food Department's grievance portal.
Step 5 – Contact the Gram Panchayat or local Ward Councillor for assistance.
Step 6 – File a complaint on the CPGRAMS portal if the district level is unresponsive.
Step 7 – Approach the Food and Civil Supplies Department at the state level.
Step 8 – File a Right to Information (RTI) application to obtain information about your ration entitlements.
required_documents: Details of dates when ration was denied

authorities.name: State Food and Civil Supplies Department

escalation_path: Fair Price Shop → Circle Supply Inspector → District Supply Officer → State Food Department → CPGRAMS
complaint_template:
